items.q: At which electrode are positive ions reduced during electrolysis?
items.correct: At the cathode (the negative electrode) — positive ions gain electrons there.
items.misconception: Positive ions are reduced at the anode because that's where positive things go.
items.random_wrong: Positive ions are oxidised at the cathode by losing protons.
items.why: Students associate 'positive' with 'attracted to positive' or get muddled by the sign conventions and switch the electrodes.

items.q: Why does water have a bent shape rather than linear?
items.correct: Oxygen has two bonding pairs and two lone pairs, giving a tetrahedral electron geometry but a bent molecular shape.
items.misconception: Water is bent because the hydrogens repel each other and push apart.
items.random_wrong: Water is bent because oxygen is heavier than hydrogen and weighs the middle down.
items.why: Students see H atoms at the ends and intuit H-H repulsion, missing that the bend is caused by lone pair-bond pair repulsion on the oxygen.

items.q: Does a catalyst affect the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst increases the yield by being consumed in the forward reaction only.
items.why: Students associate 'catalyst' with 'helps the reaction' and assume that means 'pushes towards products' rather than 'speeds up both directions equally'.

items.q: Rutherford's gold foil experiment was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1911. “1897” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Mass spectrometer development is associated with:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Aston. “Bohr” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year Rutherford proposed the nuclear atom?
items.why: The real figure is 1911 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
